era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in terminal decline; Alaric I leads the Visigoths and will sack Rome in 410 CE. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Arcadius remains administratively intact but faces Persian and barbarian pressures. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting; the Northern and Southern Dynasties period is beginning. Christianity is now the dominant religion across the Mediterranean world following Constantine's conversion and the Council of Nicaea (325 CE). This is a pivotal moment of civilisational transition in Europe and Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (Germanic and Celtic regions; oral tradition only)

events.title: Alaric I proclaimed King of the Visigoths
events.description: Alaric I, a Gothic military commander within the Roman army, is proclaimed king by his followers, formalising Visigothic independence from Roman authority. This marks a critical moment in the fragmentation of Roman military control and the rise of barbarian kingdoms within former imperial territory. Alaric will lead raids into Greece and eventually march on Italy, culminating in the sack of Rome in 410 CE. His elevation signals the shift from 'barbarian foederati' (allied troops) to autonomous political entities.

sources.name: Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, drawing on earlier sources)
Jordanes, Getica (6th century compilation of Gothic history)

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Alaric's proclamation is debated; sources place it between 395–401 CE. 400 CE is a conventional date used by modern historians, but contemporary documentation is absent. The event itself is well-attested in later sources.
cross_references: 395-001 (Division of Roman Empire under Arcadius and Honorius)

events.title: Stilicho's military campaigns against Alaric in Greece
events.description: Stilicho, the half-Vandal Roman general and regent for the Western Roman Emperor Honorius, leads military campaigns to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces in Greece. Stilicho achieves tactical successes but fails to eliminate the Gothic threat, partly due to political divisions in the Western Empire and his own precarious position. These campaigns represent the last major Roman military effort to maintain control of the Balkans and Greece against barbarian incursion.

sources.name: Claudian, panegyric poems on Stilicho (contemporary or near-contemporary, c. 400–404 CE)

events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, though they are propagandistic in nature. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty fragmentation accelerates in China
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty, which had ruled southern China since 317 CE, enters its final phase of collapse. Regional warlords and military strongmen increasingly challenge central authority. The period of the Northern and Southern Dynasties (420–589 CE) is imminent. This fragmentation reflects broader instability following the decline of Han-era centralised authority and the rise of competing regional powers in post-classical China.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song Dynasty, b. 363 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Eastern Jin decline is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 400 CE are less precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo completes The City of God (composition period)
events.description: Augustine of Hippo, one of the most influential Christian theologians, is in the process of writing The City of God (De civitate Dei), a monumental work of Christian apologetics and philosophy begun after the Visigothic sack of Rome in 410 CE. While the work's composition extends to c. 426 CE, Augustine's theological output in 400 CE reflects the intellectual ferment of late antique Christianity. His writings profoundly shape medieval and early modern Christian thought.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and bishop, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)
Possidius, Life of Augustine (written early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: Augustine's life and works are extensively documented. The City of God was begun after 410 CE, so this entry refers to Augustine's broader theological activity in 400 CE rather than the specific work.
cross_references: 410-001 (Sack of Rome, which prompted The City of God)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Alaric's proclamation as king to 400 CE is not universally accepted; some scholars place it as late as 401 CE or as early as 395 CE. The significance attributed to this event in narratives of 'barbarian invasion' has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the continuity of Roman military structures and the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative systems rather than a simple 'invasion' narrative. Augustine's major works were not yet written in 400 CE; attributing intellectual significance to this year based on works composed later risks anachronism.
historiographic_note: The year 400 CE falls at a critical juncture in Western historiography: the transition from classical antiquity to the early medieval period. However, documentation is uneven. Roman sources (Claudian, Zosimus) are biased toward military and political elites and toward the Western Empire's perspective. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. African and Asian sources outside the Roman and Chinese spheres are sparse. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated 19th- and 20th-century historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse. Surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and demographic changes are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Alaric's proclamation as king in 400 CE directly led to his march on Rome and the sack of 410 CE
Stilicho's campaigns against Alaric occurred in the same period as Alaric's consolidation of power
The fragmentation of Eastern Jin in 400 CE culminated in the founding of Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE
Augustine's theological work was part of the broader Christian intellectual response to the crisis of the Western Empire

era_context: The Western Roman Empire under Honorius and regent Stilicho is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric's Visigoths and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is in its final decades of decline. Christianity continues to consolidate institutional power across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of acute military and political instability in the Mediterranean and Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary written sources)

events.title: Stilicho defeats Alaric at the Battle of the Frigidus River (or nearby engagement)
events.description: Stilicho, the Roman general and regent, achieves a significant military victory against Alaric's Visigothic forces, likely in the region of the Frigidus River (modern-day Slovenia/Croatia border). This victory temporarily halts Visigothic expansion and demonstrates Roman military capability, though it does not resolve the underlying political crisis. The battle is part of a series of engagements between 395–410 CE in which Stilicho attempts to contain barbarian threats while managing internal Roman political divisions.
events.certainty_note: The exact date and location of this engagement are debated. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence but are propagandistic. The battle may have occurred in 399 or 400 CE.
cross_references: 394-001 (Battle of the Frigidus River, 394 CE, which may be confused with this engagement)

events.title: Gainas' revolt and massacre of Gothic foederati in Constantinople
events.description: Gainas, a Gothic military commander in the Eastern Roman army, leads a revolt against the Eastern Roman government. The revolt is suppressed, and a massacre of Gothic soldiers and settlers in Constantinople follows. This event demonstrates the acute tensions between barbarian military elites integrated into the Roman army and the Roman civilian and ecclesiastical establishment. The massacre represents a violent rejection of Gothic military dominance in the Eastern Empire, contrasting with the Western Empire's more gradual accommodation of barbarian kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in multiple sources, though they are not contemporary. The exact date is sometimes given as 400 CE; 399 CE is also plausible.
cross_references: 400-001 (Alaric's proclamation as king in the West, contemporary with Eastern instability)

events.title: Faxian, Chinese Buddhist monk, begins pilgrimage to India
events.description: Faxian (法顯), a Chinese Buddhist monk, departs on a pilgrimage to India to obtain Buddhist scriptures and study at major monasteries. His journey, which will last until c. 412 CE, is one of the most significant early Buddhist pilgrimages and provides invaluable documentation of Central Asian and Indian geography, politics, and religious life in the early 5th century. Faxian's account (the Foguoji or 'Record of Buddhist Kingdoms') is a primary source for understanding the Silk Road and Buddhist networks.
key_figures: Faxian (Buddhist monk and pilgrim, c. 337–422 CE)

sources.name: Faxian's own account, Foguoji (Record of Buddhist Kingdoms), compiled after his return

events.certainty_note: The date of Faxian's departure is traditionally given as 399 CE, though some sources suggest 400 CE. The pilgrimage itself is well-documented in his own account.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Emperor Longxu's reign continues amid factional strife
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty under Emperor Longxu (r. 397–402 CE) faces increasing internal factional conflict among military strongmen and aristocratic families. The imperial court's authority is eroding as regional warlords consolidate power. This period of instability will culminate in the dynasty's collapse and the founding of the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. The fragmentation reflects broader post-Han patterns of regional autonomy and the rise of military aristocracies.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song, b. 363 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The broad political trajectory is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 399 CE are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Gainas' revolt is disputed; some sources place it in 400 CE rather than 399 CE. The significance of Gainas' revolt has been reinterpreted by modern scholarship: rather than a simple 'barbarian uprising,' it reflects complex factional politics within the Eastern Roman elite, with Gothic military commanders as one faction among many competing for power. The traditional narrative of 'barbarian invasion' obscures the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative and military structures. Faxian's pilgrimage date of 399 CE is conventional but not universally accepted; some sources suggest 400 CE.
historiographic_note: The year 399 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Most information comes from later compilations (Zosimus, Socrates Scholasticus) written 50–150 years after the events. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic panegyrics. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. The Eastern Roman Empire's perspective is filtered through ecclesiastical sources (John Chrysostom, Socrates Scholasticus), which emphasise religious and moral dimensions over political and military detail. Faxian's pilgrimage is well-documented in his own account, but this is a retrospective compilation. The year 399 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events occurring in this specific year is difficult to assess.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's victory in 399 CE was part of his broader campaign against Alaric continuing into 400 CE
Gainas' revolt in the East occurred during the same period as Alaric's consolidation in the West
Faxian's pilgrimage beginning in 399 CE culminated in his return to China in 412 CE
The factional strife in Eastern Jin in 399 CE led directly to the dynasty's collapse and Liu Song's founding in 420 CE

era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute military and political crisis. Stilicho continues campaigns against Alaric and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal factional conflict. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting. Christianity is now institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of rapid transformation in political structures, military organisation, and religious authority across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho's military operations in the Balkans and Greece
events.description: Stilicho continues his military campaigns to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces and other barbarian groups in the Balkans and Greece. These operations are part of a sustained effort to maintain Roman military control over the eastern Balkans and prevent barbarian incursion into Italy. The campaigns are partially successful tactically but fail to resolve the underlying political crisis of barbarian integration and Roman military fragmentation.
events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, but the exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain. The year 398 CE is a conventional date for Stilicho's operations in this period.

events.title: John Chrysostom becomes Patriarch of Constantinople
events.description: John Chrysostom (c. 347–407 CE), a renowned Christian theologian and orator, is appointed Patriarch of Constantinople, the highest ecclesiastical office in the Eastern Roman Empire. His appointment marks a significant moment in the consolidation of Christian institutional power and the integration of the Church into imperial governance. Chrysostom's tenure is marked by his efforts to reform clerical discipline and his conflicts with the imperial court, particularly with the Empress Eudoxia. His appointment reflects the Church's growing political influence in late antique society.
key_figures: John Chrysostom (Patriarch of Constantinople, c. 347–407 CE)

sources.name: Palladius, Dialogue on the Life of John Chrysostom (early 5th century)
John Chrysostom's own writings and homilies (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE is well-documented in multiple sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Liu Yu gains military prominence
events.description: Liu Yu (劉裕, 363–422 CE), a military commander of humble origins, begins to gain prominence in the Eastern Jin court. Over the next two decades, Liu Yu will consolidate military power and eventually found the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. His rise exemplifies the pattern of military strongmen superseding aristocratic authority in late Eastern Jin politics. Liu Yu's career marks the transition from the Eastern Jin to the Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
key_figures: Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song Dynasty, 363–422 CE)
Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Liu Yu's rise to prominence is well-documented in Chinese historiography, though the specific date of 398 CE for his early prominence is conventional.
cross_references: 420-001 (Founding of Liu Song Dynasty by Liu Yu)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Yazdegerd I: consolidation of power
events.description: Yazdegerd I (r. 399–421 CE, though his reign may have begun slightly earlier) consolidates power in the Sassanid Persian Empire. His reign is marked by administrative reforms, religious tolerance (including toward Christians and Zoroastrians), and military campaigns against the Eastern Roman Empire and Central Asian nomads. Yazdegerd I is one of the most significant Sassanid rulers and represents a period of Persian imperial strength during the late antique period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (9th century compilation)

events.certainty_note: The date of Yazdegerd I's accession is debated; some sources place it in 399 CE, others in 401 CE. The Pahlavi sources are contemporary but fragmentary. Byzantine sources are later and biased.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch to 398 CE is secure, but the significance attributed to his appointment in narratives of 'Church triumphalism' has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the conflicts and limitations of ecclesiastical power in the late antique period. Chrysostom's conflicts with the imperial court and his eventual exile (407 CE) demonstrate the fragility of clerical authority. The dating of Yazdegerd I's accession is disputed; some sources place it as late as 401 CE. Liu Yu's early prominence in 398 CE is a conventional date; the sources do not precisely date his rise.
historiographic_note: The year 398 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic. Ecclesiastical sources (Socrates Scholasticus, Palladius) provide information on John Chrysostom but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sassanid sources are fragmentary and often preserved only in later Islamic compilations. The year 398 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's operations in 398 CE continued into the campaigns of 399 CE
John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE led to his conflicts with the imperial court and eventual exile in 407 CE
Liu Yu's rise to prominence in 398 CE culminated in his founding of Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE
Yazdegerd I's consolidation of power in 398 CE was part of his broader reign from 399–421 CE

era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute crisis. Stilicho is regent for the young Emperor Honorius and is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting. Christianity is institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of rapid political, military, and religious transformation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho becomes regent for Emperor Honorius
events.description: Stilicho, a half-Vandal Roman general, becomes regent and guardian for the young Emperor Honorius (r. 393–423 CE), who is only about 10 years old at this time. Stilicho's regency marks a critical moment in the Western Roman Empire's political fragmentation. As regent, Stilicho wields enormous power but faces constant political opposition from the Roman aristocracy and military rivals. His regency will last until his execution in 408 CE. Stilicho's position exemplifies the rise of military strongmen and the decline of traditional aristocratic authority in the late Western Empire.
key_figures: Stilicho (Roman general and regent, c. 359–408 CE)

sources.name: Orosius, History Against the Pagans (early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: Stilicho's appointment as regent is well-documented in multiple sources. The date of 397 CE is secure.

events.title: Alaric I and the Visigoths: foederati status and military integration
events.description: Alaric I and his Visigothic followers are formally integrated into the Eastern Roman military as foederati (allied troops) under a treaty with the Eastern Roman government. This arrangement grants the Visigoths land and military pay in exchange for military service. However, the arrangement is unstable, as the Eastern Roman government frequently fails to pay the agreed stipends, leading to Visigothic raids and grievances. This integration of barbarian military groups into the Roman army is a defining feature of late antique military organisation, though it ultimately contributes to the fragmentation of Roman military authority.
events.certainty_note: The foederati arrangement is well-attested, but the exact date of the treaty is debated. 397 CE is a conventional date, but some sources place it in 395 or 396 CE.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Emperor Longxu ascends to the throne
events.description: Emperor Longxu (陇西王, r. 397–402 CE) ascends to the throne of the Eastern Jin Dynasty. His reign is marked by increasing factional strife among military strongmen and aristocratic families. The imperial court's authority continues to erode as regional warlords consolidate power. Longxu's reign represents the final phase of Eastern Jin political authority before the dynasty's collapse in 420 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Emperor Longxu's accession in 397 CE is well-documented in Chinese historiography. The date is secure.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo: ordination as priest and early theological work
events.description: Augustine of Hippo, one of the most influential Christian theologians, is ordained as a priest in 397 CE. This marks the beginning of his formal ecclesiastical career, which will culminate in his appointment as bishop of Hippo in 395 CE (or possibly 396 CE, with the priesthood following in 397 CE). Augustine's theological output during this period is substantial and will profoundly influence medieval and early modern Christian thought. His works on grace, free will, and the nature of the Church are foundational to Christian theology.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and priest, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Augustine's ordination is debated; sources place it between 395–397 CE. The traditional date is 397 CE, but some scholars argue for 395 or 396 CE.
cross_references: 395-001 (Augustine's appointment as bishop of Hippo, or possibly 396 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Stilicho's regency as a period of Roman military strength has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the fragility of his position and the underlying military and political fragmentation of the Western Empire. Stilicho's reliance on barbarian troops and his conflicts with the Roman aristocracy demonstrate the limits of his authority. The foederati arrangement with Alaric is often portrayed as a 'barbarian invasion' in traditional historiography, but modern scholarship emphasises the continuity of Roman military structures and the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative systems. The exact date of Alaric's foederati status is disputed; some sources place it in 395 or 396 CE rather than 397 CE. Augustine's ordination date is debated; the traditional date of 397 CE is not universally accepted.
historiographic_note: The year 397 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic panegyrics. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Ecclesiastical sources provide information on Augustine but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. The year 397 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated earlier historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's regency beginning in 397 CE culminated in his execution in 408 CE
The foederati arrangement with Alaric in 397 CE led to his proclamation as king in 400 CE
Emperor Longxu's reign beginning in 397 CE ended with his death in 402 CE
Augustine's ordination in 397 CE was part of his broader theological career, which included responses to the crisis of the Western Empire

era_context: The Western Roman Empire under Honorius and regent Stilicho is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric's Visigoths and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is in its final decades. Christianity is institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of acute military, political, and religious transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho's military campaign against Alaric in Greece
events.description: Stilicho, the Western Roman general and regent, leads a military campaign into Greece to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces. The campaign is partially successful, with Stilicho achieving tactical victories, but fails to eliminate the Gothic threat or resolve the underlying political crisis. The campaign demonstrates both the capability and the limitations of Roman military power in the late 4th century. Alaric's forces are driven back but not destroyed, and the Visigoths will continue to pose a threat to the Eastern Roman Empire.
events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, but the exact chronology of Stilicho's campaigns is uncertain. The year 396 CE is a conventional date for this campaign.

events.title: Arcadius and the Eastern Roman court: factional conflict and power consolidation
events.description: The Eastern Roman Emperor Arcadius (r. 395–408 CE) consolidates power in Constantinople amid factional conflict between various court factions, including the praetorian prefect Rufinus and other aristocratic and military elites. Arcadius is a weak emperor, and real power is wielded by court factions and military commanders. This period of factional conflict will continue throughout Arcadius' reign and will contribute to the Eastern Empire's military and political instability.
events.certainty_note: The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court is well-attested, but the specific events of 396 CE are not precisely documented.
cross_references: 395-001 (Division of Roman Empire under Arcadius and Honorius)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: factional strife and military consolidation
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty faces increasing factional strife among military strongmen and aristocratic families. Regional warlords consolidate power at the expense of the imperial court. This period of instability will culminate in the dynasty's collapse in 420 CE. The fragmentation reflects broader post-Han patterns of regional autonomy and the rise of military aristocracies in Chinese politics.
key_figures: Emperor An of Eastern Jin (r. 396–418 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The broad political trajectory is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 396 CE are less precisely dated. Emperor An's accession is traditionally dated to 396 CE.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo: appointment as bishop of Hippo (or 395 CE)
events.description: Augustine of Hippo is appointed bishop of Hippo Regius in North Africa. This appointment marks the beginning of his formal ecclesiastical leadership and his most productive period of theological writing. As bishop, Augustine will engage in theological controversies (particularly against Donatism and Pelagianism) and will write extensively on Christian doctrine, ethics, and the nature of the Church. His appointment is a significant moment in the consolidation of Christian institutional authority in North Africa.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and bishop, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Augustine's appointment as bishop is debated; sources place it between 395–397 CE. The traditional date is 395 CE, but 396 CE is also plausible.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Stilicho's campaign in 396 CE as a major Roman military victory has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the campaign's limited success and the underlying fragmentation of Roman military authority. Stilicho's reliance on barbarian troops and his conflicts with the Roman aristocracy demonstrate the limits of his power. The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court is often portrayed as a sign of imperial weakness, but modern scholarship emphasises the complexity of late antique court politics and the agency of various factions. The exact date of Augustine's appointment as bishop is disputed; the traditional date of 395 CE is not universally accepted, and 396 or 397 CE are also plausible.
historiographic_note: The year 396 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Ecclesiastical sources provide information on Augustine but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. The year 396 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated earlier historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's campaign in 396 CE led to his appointment as regent in 397 CE
The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court in 396 CE contributed to Gainas' revolt in 399 CE
The factional strife in Eastern Jin in 396 CE led to the dynasty's collapse and Liu Song's founding in 420 CE
Augustine's appointment as bishop in 396 CE was part of his broader ecclesiastical career until his death in 430 CE
